title: Cluster Networking, CNI, NetworkPolicy, Dual Stack, and Layered Troubleshooting · Cluster Case: K8s Default Deny Dns Egress
summary: A multi-tier application has working DNS but failed connections, an additive policy gap, asymmetric dual-stack behavior, and a provider-specific data plane. Planned artifact: Add and verify the k8s default deny dns egress increment in network intent and isolation evidence policy. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Build default-deny baselines while explicitly allowing DNS, required dependencies, control paths, metadata restrictions, and tested egress destinations.
Derive ingress and egress isolation from Pod and namespace selectors, ipBlock, ports, peers, policy types, and additive allow rules.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# kubernetes-basics cumulative orchestration portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.

version-baseline:
  kubernetes: 1.36.2
  minikube: 1.38.1
  gateway-api: 1.5.1
  helm: 4.2.2
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-cluster

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: A worked example Pod can resolve DNS but cannot reach a backend after default deny; the policy selects only ingress, misses egress DNS, and assumes NetworkPolicy implementation behavior. Trace source, destination, port, direction, selector, CNI, and node boundary. Competency probe: Build default-deny baselines while explicitly allowing DNS, required dependencies, control paths, metadata restrictions, and tested egress destinations. A defensible solution applies K8s Default Deny Dns Egress, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to network intent and isolation evidence policy.

content.code:
# Worked focus: k8s-default-deny-dns-egress — Build default-deny baselines while explicitly allowing DNS, required dependencies, control paths, metadata restrictions, and tested egress destinations.
  # Evidence: k8s-worked-k8s-network-model-policy-troubleshooting-5
  - competency: k8s-worked-k8s-network-model-policy-troubleshooting-5
    artifact: network-policy.yaml
    decision: worked k8s network model policy troubleshooting 5
    environment: managed canary cluster with provider-specific load balancing
    fault-injection: drop one metrics series during an autoscaling surge
    retained-proof: accessible incident timeline with causal regression evidence
    case-key: worked5
    change: |
      pod-network: cni-implementation-boundary
      ip-family-policy: PreferDualStack
      ingress: default-deny-plus-gateway
      egress: default-deny-plus-dns-and-api
      metadata-endpoint: denied
      return-path: stateful-implementation-verified
    verify: kubectl get networkpolicy,pod,service,endpointslice -n intake -o json
    failure: Reject policy override semantics, automatic return-path claims, DNS-success as endpoint proof, or a CNI-independent packet conclusion.
    repair: Derive additive ingress and egress allows, preserve DNS and required control paths, then test name, endpoint, route, policy, and application layers.
content.caption: Runnable Kubernetes object and transfer-evidence contract example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

content.title: Non-worked case
content.text: Default-deny ingress also blocks all egress and DNS without an egress policy. This fails the K8s Default Deny Dns Egress contract because it omits a precondition, invariant, ownership rule, or counterexample needed by this exact case.
